---
title: "The Empirical CDF and Sampling With Replacement"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the true curve / theoretical value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the mean
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas / bands


def ecdf(sample):
    """Return (xs, Fs): the sorted distinct step locations and the empirical
    CDF value just *after* each step. Pure bookkeeping — the very object this
    article defines — used only to draw the pictures."""
    xs = np.sort(sample)
    n = xs.size
    Fs = np.arange(1, n + 1) / n
    return xs, Fs
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the first of the two articles that close the
> Probability Foundations track by building the **objects that sampling theory consumes**.
> It rests directly on three earlier results and uses each of them by name. From
> [PROB-02 *Random Variables*] it takes the **cumulative distribution function**
> $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ as the one object that describes *any* random variable — here we learn
> to *estimate* that object from data. From [PROB-08 *Independence and the i.i.d. Sample*]
> it takes the **i.i.d. sample**: $n$ draws from one distribution, independent and
> identically distributed. From [PROB-11 *Bernoulli, Binomial & Negative-Binomial*] it
> takes the fact that **a sum of independent indicator variables is Binomial**, which is
> what makes the empirical CDF's sampling behavior exactly computable. The convergence
> statement at the end is phrased in the vocabulary of [PROB-21 *Modes of Convergence*]. It
> sets up, across the track boundary, the **bootstrap** ([INFR] track, owned by the
> Statistical Inference article on resampling) and the resampling that powers **bagging**
> in the Classification and Unsupervised Learning tracks. This track *builds the object and
> licenses the resampling*; the downstream tracks build the *methods* on top of it.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

You have a finite sample — $n$ numbers, drawn independently from some unknown distribution
$F$ — and you want $F$ itself: the whole curve, $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ for every $x$. You do not
get $F$. But you get the single best guess the data can make about it, and it is almost
embarrassingly simple: **the fraction of your data at or below $x$**. That step function is
the **empirical CDF**, $\hat F_n$. This article defines it, proves that at each fixed point
it is an unbiased estimate of $F(x)$ with variance $F(x)\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)/n$ — because the
count of points below $x$ is exactly a Binomial — and shows that it converges to $F$ not
just pointwise but *uniformly*, the Glivenko–Cantelli theorem. The single claim the article
defends is that **$\hat F_n$ is a distribution in its own right** — a legitimate
probability distribution that puts mass $1/n$ on each observed point — so that "drawing a
fresh sample from the data, with replacement" is not a trick but literally *sampling from
$\hat F_n$*. That one observation is the entire theoretical foundation of resampling, and
the reason the bootstrap is allowed to exist.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The data's own best guess at the unknown distribution $F$ is the **empirical CDF**
$$
\hat F_n(x) \;=\; \frac{\#\{i : X_i \le x\}}{n} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf 1\{X_i \le x\},
$$
the fraction of the sample at or below $x$. It is a genuine CDF: a right-continuous step
function climbing from $0$ to $1$ in jumps of $1/n$. The distribution it describes places
probability $1/n$ on each observed value — so **sampling with replacement from your data
is exactly sampling from $\hat F_n$**. Because $\hat F_n(x)$ counts i.i.d. yes/no events, it
is a scaled Binomial: unbiased for $F(x)$, with variance $F(x)(1-F(x))/n$ that vanishes as
$n$ grows, and (Glivenko–Cantelli) the whole curve converges *uniformly* to $F$.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. Two ideas live inside it and they reinforce each other.
The first is **estimation**: $\hat F_n$ is what you write down when someone hands you data
and asks "what distribution did this come from?" and you refuse to assume a parametric
shape. The second is **resampling**: once you accept that $\hat F_n$ is a real
distribution, you can *draw from it*, and drawing from it means picking observed data points
uniformly at random — with replacement, because each draw is independent and $\hat F_n$
does not change between draws. The bridge from "estimate of $F$" to "thing I can sample
from" is the whole point, and it is what makes the last third of the article — and the
entire bootstrap that follows in the Inference track — possible.

# Why we are here: the problem of the unknown distribution {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **we want $F$, we cannot have $F$, so what
do we use instead — and what is that substitute good for?** Every step below answers a piece
of that.

## The problem before there was an answer

Almost everything in statistics is a statement about a distribution we never see. A clinician
wants the distribution of recovery times; an engineer wants the distribution of component
lifetimes; an economist wants the distribution of household incomes. In each case there is a
true $F$ — the function $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ from [PROB-02] that fully describes the random
quantity — and in each case all we are handed is a finite, noisy sample drawn from it.

For a long stretch of statistical history the only move available was to *assume the shape*.
Declare the data normal, or exponential, or Poisson, estimate the one or two parameters of
that family, and proceed. This is the **parametric** program, and when the shape assumption
is right it is wonderfully efficient — two numbers ($\mu,\sigma^2$) stand in for an entire
curve. But it pays for that efficiency with a standing risk: *if the assumed shape is wrong,
every downstream answer is wrong*, and the data themselves were never consulted about the
shape.

The question that forced the empirical CDF into existence is the obvious dual: **can the data
describe their own distribution, without us guessing its form first?** Can we let the sample
draw the curve? The answer, and it is one of the cleanest ideas in the subject, is yes — and
the construction is so direct that the only surprise is how much it can do.

## The honest dual: assume nothing about the shape

The empirical CDF is the **nonparametric** answer. "Nonparametric" does not mean "no
parameters"; it means we refuse to commit to a finite-dimensional family of shapes. Instead
of "$F$ is a normal with these two unknowns," we say "$F$ is *some* distribution, and our
estimate of it is whatever the data literally show." The estimate will turn out to have $n$
'parameters' — one per data point — which is exactly the sense in which it assumes nothing
about the form.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between a parametric and a nonparametric estimate of a
distribution, and when would you reach for each?"* A **parametric** estimate fixes a family
(say $\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$) and estimates its few parameters; it is efficient and gives
smooth, extrapolatable curves *when the family is right*, and badly biased when it is wrong.
A **nonparametric** estimate like $\hat F_n$ assumes only that an $F$ exists and lets the
data dictate the shape; it is robust to misspecification and asymptotically correct for *any*
$F$, but it is rougher (a step function), it never extrapolates beyond the observed range,
and it needs more data to reach the same precision. Reach for parametric when you have a
principled reason to trust the shape (a physical mechanism, a limit theorem) and little data;
reach for the empirical CDF when you distrust the shape, have a reasonable amount of data, or
specifically want to *resample* — which, as we will see in @sec-resampling, is the property
that makes the bootstrap run.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture the sample as a handful of pebbles dropped on the number line: $X_1,\dots,X_n$, $n$
positions, possibly with ties. Now walk left to right and keep a running tally: "what
fraction of the pebbles have I passed (or am I standing on) so far?" That running fraction,
read off as you sweep $x$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, *is* the empirical CDF. It starts at
$0$ (no pebbles passed), and every time you step onto a pebble it jumps up by $1/n$ (or by
$k/n$ if $k$ pebbles share that spot), and it ends at $1$ once every pebble is behind you.

Compare that to the true $F$, which (if $F$ is continuous) is a smooth curve climbing from
$0$ to $1$. The empirical CDF is its **staircase shadow**: same overall ascent, same
endpoints, but built out of $n$ flat treads and $n$ vertical risers instead of a smooth
slope. The more pebbles you drop, the finer the treads, and the staircase hugs the smooth
curve ever more tightly. @fig-ecdf-intuition shows exactly this for a sample drawn from a
known $F$, so you can see the staircase chasing the curve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ecdf-intuition
#| fig-cap: "The empirical CDF as a staircase shadow of the true CDF. A sample of $n=15$ points (ticks on the axis) is drawn from a standard normal. The blue step function is $\\hat F_n$: it starts at 0, jumps by $1/n$ at each observation, and ends at 1. The red curve is the true $F$ it is trying to recover. Each jump sits directly above its data point. With only 15 points the staircase is coarse, but it already tracks the overall climb of the true curve."
from scipy import stats

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 15
sample = rng.normal(0, 1, size=n)
xs, Fs = ecdf(sample)

grid = np.linspace(-3.2, 3.2, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(grid, stats.norm.cdf(grid), color=LINE, label=r"true $F(x)=\Phi(x)$")
# draw the empirical CDF as an explicit staircase
ax.step(np.concatenate([[grid[0]], xs, [grid[-1]]]),
        np.concatenate([[0], Fs, [1]]),
        where="post", color=POINT, label=r"empirical $\hat F_n(x)$")
ax.scatter(xs, Fs, color=POINT, s=22, zorder=4)
ax.plot(sample, np.full_like(sample, -0.03), "|", color=ACCENT,
        markersize=14, markeredgewidth=1.6, label="the sample")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("probability $\\le x$")
ax.set_ylim(-0.06, 1.05)
ax.set_title("A 15-point empirical CDF chasing the true curve")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

There are two ways to read this same picture, and holding both at once is the key to the
article. **Read it vertically** and $\hat F_n$ is an *estimator*: at any fixed $x$, its
height is our guess at $F(x)$. **Read it as an object** and the staircase *is a probability
distribution*: the jumps are a probability mass function placing weight $1/n$ on each data
point. The first reading leads to @sec-formal (bias, variance, convergence); the second
leads to @sec-resampling (sampling from the data itself). They are the same staircase.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The indicator function, built from scratch {#sec-indicator}

The definition rests on one small device — the **indicator function** — so we build it
explicitly rather than assume it. For any statement $A$ that is either true or false, define

$$
\mathbf 1\{A\} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if $A$ is true},\\[2pt]
0 & \text{if $A$ is false}.
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-indicator}

That is the entire definition: a switch that reports $1$ for "yes" and $0$ for "no". The
statement we will plug in is "$X_i \le x$". So $\mathbf 1\{X_i \le x\}$ is $1$ when the $i$-th
data point lands at or below the threshold $x$, and $0$ otherwise.

The reason the indicator is the right tool is that **summing indicators counts**. If you add
up $\mathbf 1\{X_i \le x\}$ over all $i$, every "yes" contributes $1$ and every "no"
contributes $0$, so the sum is precisely the number of data points at or below $x$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf 1\{X_i \le x\} \;=\; \#\{\,i : X_i \le x\,\}.
$$ {#eq-count}

This identity — *counting is summing indicators* — is the hinge of the whole article. It is
what lets us pour the machinery of expectation and variance, built for sums, onto a thing
that looks like a count.

## The definition {#sec-definition}

::: {#def-ecdf}
**Definition (empirical CDF).** Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be a sample of real numbers. The
**empirical cumulative distribution function** is the function
$$
\hat F_n(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf 1\{X_i \le x\},
\qquad x \in \mathbb R .
$$ {#eq-ecdf-def}
Equivalently, by @eq-count, $\hat F_n(x)$ is the proportion of the sample at or below $x$.
:::

Every symbol earns its place. The sum $\sum_{i=1}^n$ runs over all $n$ data points; each
term is the on/off switch of @eq-indicator for that point; dividing by $n$ turns the count
into a proportion, a number in $[0,1]$. The hat marks it as something computed *from data*,
to distinguish it from the true, unhatted $F$. The subscript $n$ records the sample size,
because — crucially — $\hat F_n$ is itself random and changes with every new sample.

Before trusting $\hat F_n$ as a stand-in for $F$, we should check it really *is* a CDF.
Recall from [PROB-02] that a function qualifies as a cumulative distribution function exactly
when it has three properties: it is non-decreasing, it is right-continuous, and it tends to
$0$ as $x\to-\infty$ and to $1$ as $x\to+\infty$. We verify all three, because a "distribution
estimate" that was not a distribution would be a fraud.

::: {#lem-ecdf-is-cdf}
**Lemma ($\hat F_n$ is a valid CDF).** For every sample, the function $\hat F_n$ of
@eq-ecdf-def is non-decreasing, right-continuous, and satisfies $\hat F_n(x)\to 0$ as
$x\to-\infty$ and $\hat F_n(x)\to 1$ as $x\to+\infty$.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Non-decreasing.** Take any $x_1 < x_2$. If a data point satisfies $X_i \le x_1$, then since
$x_1 < x_2$ it also satisfies $X_i \le x_2$. So every term that is $1$ in the sum for
$\hat F_n(x_1)$ is also $1$ in the sum for $\hat F_n(x_2)$, and possibly more terms switch on.
The count @eq-count can only stay the same or rise as $x$ increases, hence so does
$\hat F_n$. There is no algebra to hide: a larger threshold can only catch more points.

**Right-continuous.** Fix a point $x_0$ and let $x$ decrease down to $x_0$ from above. We must
show $\hat F_n(x)\to\hat F_n(x_0)$. A data point $X_i$ is counted in $\hat F_n(x)$ for $x>x_0$
iff $X_i\le x$. As $x$ shrinks toward $x_0$, the only points whose status could change are
those with $X_i$ in the shrinking interval $(x_0, x]$. Take $x$ close enough to $x_0$ that
this interval contains *no* data points (possible because there are finitely many points, so
there is a gap above $x_0$ unless a point sits exactly at $x_0$ — and a point exactly at $x_0$
satisfies $X_i\le x_0$ and is already counted in $\hat F_n(x_0)$). For such $x$ the count
equals the count at $x_0$, so $\hat F_n(x)=\hat F_n(x_0)$. Hence the limit from the right is
$\hat F_n(x_0)$: right-continuous. (The same argument shows the function is *flat* between
consecutive data points and *jumps* at the data, which is the staircase of
@fig-ecdf-intuition.)

**Limits.** Let $m=\min_i X_i$ and $M=\max_i X_i$. For any $x<m$, no data point satisfies
$X_i\le x$, so the count is $0$ and $\hat F_n(x)=0$; thus $\hat F_n(x)\to 0$ as $x\to-\infty$.
For any $x\ge M$, *every* data point satisfies $X_i\le x$, so the count is $n$ and
$\hat F_n(x)=n/n=1$; thus $\hat F_n(x)\to 1$ as $x\to+\infty$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So $\hat F_n$ is a bona fide CDF. The distribution it describes is **discrete**: it places
all its probability on the $n$ observed points, with mass $1/n$ at each (or $k/n$ at a value
hit $k$ times). That last sentence is the one we cash in for resampling in @sec-resampling.

## Each value $\hat F_n(x)$ is a scaled Binomial {#sec-binomial}

Now fix a single threshold $x$ and ask the estimation question: *how good is the number
$\hat F_n(x)$ as a guess for the true number $F(x)$?* To answer it we must treat $\hat F_n(x)$
as what it is — a random quantity, because the data are random — and find its distribution.

Here is where [PROB-08] and [PROB-11] pay off. Consider one term of the sum,
$\mathbf 1\{X_i \le x\}$. It is a single yes/no event: "did the $i$-th draw land at or below
$x$?" Its value is $1$ with probability $P(X_i\le x)$ and $0$ otherwise. But $X_i$ is drawn
from $F$, so by the very definition of the CDF,

$$
P(X_i \le x) \;=\; F(x).
$$ {#eq-success-prob}

A random variable that is $1$ with probability $p$ and $0$ with probability $1-p$ is, by
definition ([PROB-11]), a **Bernoulli$(p)$** variable. So each indicator
$\mathbf 1\{X_i \le x\}$ is a Bernoulli trial with success probability $p=F(x)$ — call its
value a "success" when the point falls at or below $x$.

Three facts about this collection of indicators, all imported by name from earlier articles:

1. They are **identically distributed**: each $X_i$ comes from the same $F$, so each
   indicator has the same success probability $F(x)$ ([PROB-08], the "identically distributed"
   half of i.i.d.).
2. They are **independent**: the draws $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are independent ([PROB-08], the
   "independent" half), and a function of $X_i$ alone (the indicator) inherits that
   independence — knowing whether $X_3$ fell below $x$ tells you nothing about whether $X_7$
   did.
3. A **sum of $n$ independent Bernoulli$(p)$ variables is Binomial$(n,p)$** — this is exactly
   the result built in [PROB-11], where the Binomial pmf was derived as the distribution of
   the number of successes in $n$ independent trials.

Let $S_n(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf 1\{X_i \le x\}$ be the count from @eq-count — the number of
"successes". By the three facts,

$$
S_n(x) \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}\bigl(n,\, F(x)\bigr),
$$ {#eq-binomial}

and the empirical CDF is just that count divided by $n$:

$$
\hat F_n(x) \;=\; \frac{S_n(x)}{n}.
$$ {#eq-ecdf-as-binom}

**This is the master fact of the section.** It says the random height of the staircase at any
fixed $x$ is a Binomial coin-counting problem in disguise. Everything quantitative about
$\hat F_n(x)$ — its mean, its variance, how fast it settles down — now follows from the
Binomial, which we already understand completely.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is $\hat F_n(x)$ Binomial and not something more complicated?"* Because at a *fixed*
$x$, each observation contributes a single independent yes/no outcome — below $x$ or not —
with the same probability $F(x)$, and the empirical CDF just counts the yeses and divides by
$n$. Counting independent identical yes/no trials *is* the Binomial; there is nothing more to
it. The subtlety, which a sharp interviewer will push on next, is that this is true only at a
*single* $x$. As a whole *function* of $x$, the heights $\hat F_n(x)$ at different thresholds
are dependent (they are built from the same data and are forced to increase together), so the
process is not a bag of independent Binomials — it is a correlated family. The single-point
Binomial governs pointwise behaviour; the joint behaviour across all $x$ is what
Glivenko–Cantelli in @sec-gc controls.
:::

## Mean and variance: unbiased, and shrinking like $1/n$ {#sec-mean-var}

We now read the mean and variance of $\hat F_n(x)$ straight off the Binomial. From [PROB-11]
(and [PROB-05], [PROB-06]) we have the two standard facts about a Binomial$(n,p)$ count: its
mean is $np$ and its variance is $np(1-p)$. We will use them with $p=F(x)$, and we will also
re-derive the variance one rung lower, from the indicators, so nothing is taken on faith.

**The mean (unbiasedness).** Take the expectation of @eq-ecdf-as-binom. Expectation is linear
([PROB-05]) and $n$ is a constant, so

$$
\mathbb E\bigl[\hat F_n(x)\bigr]
= \mathbb E\!\left[\frac{S_n(x)}{n}\right]
= \frac{1}{n}\,\mathbb E\bigl[S_n(x)\bigr]
= \frac{1}{n}\cdot n\,F(x)
= F(x).
$$ {#eq-unbiased}

Here $\mathbb E[S_n(x)] = n\,F(x)$ is the Binomial mean $np$ with $p=F(x)$. The conclusion is
clean and important: **$\hat F_n(x)$ is an unbiased estimator of $F(x)$**, for every $x$, for
every sample size. On average — over many independent repetitions of the whole sampling
experiment — the staircase height at $x$ is dead-on the true curve. There is no systematic
distortion to correct for. (We can see @eq-unbiased even faster from @eq-ecdf-def directly:
each $\mathbb E[\mathbf 1\{X_i\le x\}] = P(X_i\le x) = F(x)$ because the expectation of a
$0/1$ indicator is just the probability it equals $1$; the average of $n$ copies of $F(x)$
is $F(x)$.)

**The variance.** Take the variance of @eq-ecdf-as-binom. Pulling the constant $1/n$ out of a
variance squares it ([PROB-06], $\operatorname{Var}(aX)=a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)$):

$$
\operatorname{Var}\bigl(\hat F_n(x)\bigr)
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac{S_n(x)}{n}\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\operatorname{Var}\bigl(S_n(x)\bigr)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\,F(x)\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)
= \frac{F(x)\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)}{n}.
$$ {#eq-variance}

Here $\operatorname{Var}(S_n(x))=n\,F(x)(1-F(x))$ is the Binomial variance $np(1-p)$ with
$p=F(x)$. We can also reach @eq-variance from the indicators directly, which makes the role of
independence explicit. Each indicator $\mathbf 1\{X_i\le x\}$ is Bernoulli$(F(x))$ with
variance $F(x)(1-F(x))$ ([PROB-11]: a Bernoulli$(p)$ has variance $p(1-p)$). Because the
indicators are independent, **the variance of their sum is the sum of their variances**
([PROB-08], the additivity of variance under independence):

$$
\operatorname{Var}\bigl(S_n(x)\bigr)
= \sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}\bigl(\mathbf 1\{X_i\le x\}\bigr)
= n\,F(x)\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr),
$$

and dividing by $n^2$ recovers @eq-variance. Independence is doing real work here: without it
the cross-covariance terms would not vanish and the variance would not be this clean.

Read @eq-variance for meaning, because it answers the practical question "how much data do I
need?":

- **It vanishes like $1/n$.** Quadruple the sample and the variance quarters; the standard
  deviation, $\sqrt{F(x)(1-F(x))/n}$, falls like $1/\sqrt n$ — the universal statistical
  exchange rate. To halve your uncertainty about $F(x)$, you need four times the data.
- **It is largest in the middle.** The factor $F(x)(1-F(x))$ is a downward parabola in
  $F(x)$, maximized at $F(x)=\tfrac12$ (the median region) and pinched to $0$ at the tails
  where $F(x)\to 0$ or $1$. So the empirical CDF is *most* uncertain near the median of the
  distribution and *least* uncertain far out in the tails — there is simply less room for the
  proportion to wobble when it is near $0$ or $1$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the empirical CDF a consistent estimator of $F(x)$?"* Yes, pointwise, and you can prove
it in one line from what we have. An estimator is **consistent** if it converges in
probability to the truth as $n\to\infty$ ([PROB-21]). Here $\hat F_n(x)$ is unbiased
(@eq-unbiased) with variance $F(x)(1-F(x))/n \to 0$ (@eq-variance). By **Chebyshev's
inequality** ([PROB-19]), for any $\varepsilon>0$,
$P(|\hat F_n(x)-F(x)|\ge\varepsilon)\le \operatorname{Var}(\hat F_n(x))/\varepsilon^2
= F(x)(1-F(x))/(n\varepsilon^2)\to 0$. So $\hat F_n(x)\xrightarrow{\;p\;}F(x)$: at every
fixed point, the staircase height converges in probability to the true curve. The stronger,
*uniform* statement — that the *worst* gap over all $x$ at once goes to zero — is
Glivenko–Cantelli, @sec-gc.
:::

@fig-shrink visualizes both facts at once: as $n$ grows the staircase tightens around $F$,
and a pointwise $\pm$ one-standard-deviation band (computed from @eq-variance) narrows like
$1/\sqrt n$, fattest in the middle and pinched at the tails.

```{python}
#| label: fig-shrink
#| fig-cap: "The empirical CDF converging as the sample grows. Each panel overlays $\\hat F_n$ (blue staircase) on the true exponential CDF (red), for $n=20, 100, 500$. The shaded band is the pointwise $\\pm 1$ standard deviation $\\sqrt{F(x)(1-F(x))/n}$ from @eq-variance: it shrinks like $1/\\sqrt n$ across panels, and within each panel it is widest near the median and pinched toward the tails, exactly as the factor $F(x)(1-F(x))$ predicts."
from scipy import stats

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
grid = np.linspace(0, 5, 400)
trueF = stats.expon.cdf(grid)            # exponential(1) true CDF
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.2, 3.3), sharey=True)
for ax, n in zip(axes, (20, 100, 500)):
    s = rng.exponential(1.0, size=n)
    xs, Fs = ecdf(s)
    band = np.sqrt(trueF * (1 - trueF) / n)
    ax.fill_between(grid, np.clip(trueF - band, 0, 1), np.clip(trueF + band, 0, 1),
                    color=FILL, alpha=0.55, lw=0, label=r"$\pm 1$ SD")
    ax.plot(grid, trueF, color=LINE, lw=1.8, label="true $F$")
    ax.step(np.concatenate([[grid[0]], xs, [grid[-1]]]),
            np.concatenate([[0], Fs, [1]]), where="post",
            color=POINT, lw=1.3, label=r"$\hat F_n$")
    ax.set_title(f"$n = {n}$"); ax.set_xlabel("$x$")
    ax.set_xlim(0, 5)
axes[0].set_ylabel("probability $\\le x$")
axes[0].legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Glivenko–Cantelli: the whole curve converges, all at once {#sec-gc}

Pointwise convergence (the Chebyshev argument above) says: pick any single $x$, and the
staircase height there settles on $F(x)$. That is reassuring but not quite what we want.
What we want is that the staircase converges *as a curve* — that there is no $x$, anywhere,
where it stays stubbornly far from $F$. The right way to say "the whole curve is close" is to
measure the **largest vertical gap** between the two functions, the supremum distance

$$
D_n \;=\; \sup_{x\in\mathbb R} \,\bigl|\hat F_n(x) - F(x)\bigr|,
$$ {#eq-Dn}

where $\sup$ (supremum) is the worst-case gap over every threshold $x$ at once. The
**Glivenko–Cantelli theorem** says this worst-case gap vanishes.

::: {#thm-gc}
**Theorem (Glivenko–Cantelli).** Let $X_1,X_2,\dots$ be i.i.d. with CDF $F$. Then the
empirical CDF converges *uniformly* to $F$: with probability $1$,
$$
D_n = \sup_{x}\,\bigl|\hat F_n(x)-F(x)\bigr| \xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{} 0.
$$
:::

This is sometimes called the **fundamental theorem of statistics**, and the name is earned:
it is the formal license to treat the sample as a faithful proxy for the population. Where the
pointwise result controls each $x$ separately, Glivenko–Cantelli controls them *all
simultaneously*, in the strongest convergence mode (almost-sure, from [PROB-21]).

A complete measure-theoretic proof is beyond our Calc-1 floor and belongs to the empirical-
process machinery in a graduate probability course; we will not pretend otherwise. But the
*reason it is true* is fully within reach, and we give the honest logical chain rather than
wave at it. The argument has two ingredients, both already in hand:

1. **Pointwise convergence at every $x$.** By the law of large numbers — itself a consequence
   of the convergence we sketched via Chebyshev in the previous callout — for each fixed $x$,
   $\hat F_n(x)\to F(x)$. (The LLN is owned by the Statistical Inference track; here we use
   only the pointwise convergence it delivers.)
2. **Monotonicity lets finitely many points control the whole line.** This is the clever
   step, and it is pure Calc-1 reasoning about increasing functions. Both $\hat F_n$ and $F$
   are non-decreasing and both run from $0$ to $1$. Pick a fine grid of thresholds
   $-\infty = t_0 < t_1 < \dots < t_k = +\infty$ chosen so that $F$ rises by at most
   $\varepsilon$ across each cell, i.e. $F(t_{j})-F(t_{j-1}) \le \varepsilon$ (possible for
   any $\varepsilon>0$ because $F$ climbs a total of $1$). Now consider any $x$, lying in some
   cell $t_{j-1} \le x < t_j$. Because *both* functions are increasing,
   $$
   \hat F_n(t_{j-1}) \le \hat F_n(x) \le \hat F_n(t_j),
   \qquad
   F(t_{j-1}) \le F(x) \le F(t_j).
   $$
   We now bound the signed gap $\hat F_n(x)-F(x)$ on both sides, using nothing but these two
   chains of inequalities. For the **upper** side, take the largest $\hat F_n$ and the smallest
   $F$ available in the cell:
   $$
   \hat F_n(x) - F(x) \;\le\; \hat F_n(t_j) - F(t_{j-1}).
   $$
   Add and subtract $F(t_j)$ to split this into a grid-point gap plus a cell-rise term:
   $$
   \hat F_n(t_j) - F(t_{j-1})
   = \underbrace{\bigl(\hat F_n(t_j) - F(t_j)\bigr)}_{\text{gap at the grid point }t_j}
     + \underbrace{\bigl(F(t_j) - F(t_{j-1})\bigr)}_{\le\,\varepsilon}
   \;\le\; \bigl|\hat F_n(t_j)-F(t_j)\bigr| + \varepsilon .
   $$
   For the **lower** side, take the smallest $\hat F_n$ and the largest $F$:
   $$
   \hat F_n(x) - F(x) \;\ge\; \hat F_n(t_{j-1}) - F(t_j),
   $$
   and this time add and subtract $F(t_{j-1})$:
   $$
   \hat F_n(t_{j-1}) - F(t_j)
   = \underbrace{\bigl(\hat F_n(t_{j-1}) - F(t_{j-1})\bigr)}_{\text{gap at the grid point }t_{j-1}}
     - \underbrace{\bigl(F(t_j) - F(t_{j-1})\bigr)}_{\le\,\varepsilon}
   \;\ge\; -\bigl|\hat F_n(t_{j-1})-F(t_{j-1})\bigr| - \varepsilon .
   $$
   Combining the two sides, the *absolute* gap at the arbitrary point $x$ is controlled by the
   absolute gaps at the two enclosing grid points plus $\varepsilon$:
   $$
   \bigl|\hat F_n(x) - F(x)\bigr|
   \;\le\;
   \max\bigl(\bigl|\hat F_n(t_{j-1})-F(t_{j-1})\bigr|,\;
             \bigl|\hat F_n(t_j)-F(t_j)\bigr|\bigr) + \varepsilon .
   $$
   This holds for *every* $x$ (each falls in some cell), so taking the supremum over $x$ on the
   left, the worst gap $D_n$ is at most $\varepsilon$ plus the largest gap over the *finitely
   many* grid points $t_1,\dots,t_{k-1}$:
   $$
   D_n \;\le\; \max_{1\le j\le k-1}\bigl|\hat F_n(t_j)-F(t_j)\bigr| + \varepsilon .
   $$
3. **Finitely many pointwise limits combine.** By step 1 each of those finitely many grid-point
   gaps goes to $0$; a maximum of finitely many things that each go to $0$ also goes to $0$.
   So for large $n$ the worst grid-point gap is below $\varepsilon$, making $D_n \le
   2\varepsilon$. Since $\varepsilon$ was arbitrary, $D_n\to 0$.

The single idea that makes this work — and that is worth carrying away even without the full
proof — is that **monotonicity converts a statement about infinitely many points into a
statement about a finite grid.** You only ever need to control the curve at a finite mesh,
because between mesh points an increasing function cannot wander far.

The companion quantitative result, which we cite rather than derive (its proof needs the same
empirical-process tools), pins down *how fast* $D_n$ shrinks: the
**Dvoretzky–Kiefer–Wolfowitz (DKW) inequality** states that for every $\varepsilon>0$,
$P\bigl(D_n > \varepsilon\bigr) \le 2e^{-2n\varepsilon^2}$. This is what lets you draw an
honest *uniform* confidence band around the empirical CDF — a band of half-width
$\sqrt{\ln(2/\alpha)/(2n)}$ that traps the *entire* true curve with probability $1-\alpha$.
@fig-gc shows both the shrinking worst-case gap and such a band.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gc
#| fig-cap: "Glivenko–Cantelli in action. **Left:** the worst-case gap $D_n = \\sup_x|\\hat F_n - F|$ versus sample size (log–log axes), averaged over 200 replications, with the DKW-rate reference line $\\propto 1/\\sqrt n$. The gap marches down at the $1/\\sqrt n$ rate. **Right:** a single $n=80$ sample with the 95% DKW uniform band (shaded) around $\\hat F_n$; the true $F$ (red) lies entirely inside the band, which is the band's guarantee."
from scipy import stats

rng = np.random.default_rng(202)
ns = np.array([10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 320, 640, 1280])
reps = 200
mean_D = []
for n in ns:
    Ds = []
    for _ in range(reps):
        s = np.sort(rng.normal(0, 1, size=n))
        Femp = np.arange(1, n + 1) / n
        Ftrue = stats.norm.cdf(s)
        # sup gap checked just above and below each jump
        D = np.max(np.abs(np.concatenate([Femp - Ftrue,
                                           Femp - 1.0 / n - Ftrue])))
        Ds.append(D)
    mean_D.append(np.mean(Ds))
mean_D = np.array(mean_D)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.6))
axL.loglog(ns, mean_D, "o-", color=POINT, label=r"mean $D_n$")
axL.loglog(ns, mean_D[0] * np.sqrt(ns[0] / ns), "--", color=GREY,
           label=r"$\propto 1/\sqrt{n}$ reference")
axL.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"worst-case gap $D_n$")
axL.set_title("The worst gap vanishes at rate $1/\\sqrt n$")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

n = 80
s = rng.normal(0, 1, size=n)
xs, Fs = ecdf(s)
alpha = 0.05
eps = np.sqrt(np.log(2 / alpha) / (2 * n))   # DKW half-width
grid = np.linspace(-3.2, 3.2, 400)
lo = np.clip(np.interp(grid, xs, Fs, left=0, right=1) - eps, 0, 1)
hi = np.clip(np.interp(grid, xs, Fs, left=0, right=1) + eps, 0, 1)
axR.fill_between(grid, lo, hi, color=FILL, alpha=0.55, lw=0, label="95% DKW band")
axR.step(np.concatenate([[grid[0]], xs, [grid[-1]]]),
         np.concatenate([[0], Fs, [1]]), where="post", color=POINT, label=r"$\hat F_n$")
axR.plot(grid, stats.norm.cdf(grid), color=LINE, label="true $F$")
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("probability $\\le x$")
axR.set_title(f"95% uniform band ($n={n}$)")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# The plug-in principle: estimating any feature of $F$ {#sec-plugin}

We have an estimate of the *whole distribution*. The natural next question — and the one that
makes $\hat F_n$ a workhorse rather than a curiosity — is: *can we use it to estimate
particular features of $F$*, like its mean, its variance, its median, its $90$th percentile?
The answer is a single, almost mechanical, idea.

Almost every quantity you care about is a **functional** of $F$ — a rule $\theta = T(F)$ that
takes the whole distribution and returns a number. The mean is a functional,
$T(F)=\int x\,dF(x) = \mathbb E[X]$. The variance is a functional. The median is the
functional "the $x$ where $F$ crosses $\tfrac12$". A percentile, a tail probability, a
correlation between two coordinates — all functionals of the underlying distribution.

::: {.callout-important title="The plug-in principle"}
To estimate a feature $\theta = T(F)$ of the unknown distribution, **apply the very same
functional to the empirical distribution**:
$$
\hat\theta \;=\; T\bigl(\hat F_n\bigr).
$$
You estimate "a property of $F$" by computing "that exact property of the data's own
distribution $\hat F_n$." No new formula per feature — one principle covers them all.
:::

The principle is so natural it can feel like a tautology, but it has real content, and it has
a recurring consequence worth internalizing: **because $\hat F_n$ puts mass $1/n$ on each data
point, plugging it into a functional turns integrals against $dF$ into averages over the
data.** Let us make this concrete with the mean, because it explains why the plug-in estimate
of the mean is exactly the familiar sample average.

The mean functional is $T(F) = \int x\,dF(x)$, the expectation of $X$ under $F$. The empirical
distribution $\hat F_n$ is the discrete distribution with probability $1/n$ at each $X_i$. The
expectation under a discrete distribution is the sum of (value $\times$ probability) over its
support ([PROB-05]). Therefore

$$
\hat\theta = T(\hat F_n)
= \int x \, d\hat F_n(x)
= \sum_{i=1}^n X_i \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{n}}_{\text{mass at }X_i}
= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i
= \bar X.
$$ {#eq-plugin-mean}

The plug-in estimate of the population mean *is* the sample mean — not by a separate
definition, but as the forced output of the plug-in principle. The same machine, fed the
variance functional, returns $\frac1n\sum (X_i-\bar X)^2$; fed the median functional, it
returns the sample median; fed the $q$-quantile functional, the sample $q$-quantile. One
principle, every summary statistic.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the plug-in estimator always unbiased?"* No — and this is the honest catch a sharp
examiner wants. The plug-in mean $\bar X$ happens to be unbiased, but plug-in is *not*
unbiasedness-preserving in general. The classic example is variance: the plug-in variance
$\frac1n\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2$ uses divisor $n$, and its expectation is
$\frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2$, *not* $\sigma^2$ — it is biased low, which is the entire reason the
"sample variance" people quote uses divisor $n-1$ (Bessel's correction). The plug-in
principle gives a *consistent* estimator for any reasonable functional (it inherits
$\hat F_n\to F$), but consistency is an $n\to\infty$ promise; finite-sample bias is a separate
question that has to be checked functional by functional. Knowing that plug-in buys
consistency but not unbiasedness is exactly the distinction interviewers probe.
:::

# Sampling with replacement: drawing from the data itself {#sec-resampling}

We have spent the article reading the staircase *vertically*, as an estimator. Now we read it
*as an object*, and collect the payoff promised in the Core. This is the conceptual hinge of
the whole article and the bridge to everything downstream.

## $\hat F_n$ is a distribution you can draw from

We proved in @lem-ecdf-is-cdf that $\hat F_n$ is a genuine CDF, describing the discrete
distribution that puts probability mass $1/n$ on each observed value $X_1,\dots,X_n$. A
distribution you can write down is a distribution you can **sample from**. So ask: what does
it *mean* to generate a fresh draw $X^*$ from $\hat F_n$?

By construction $\hat F_n$ assigns $P(X^* = X_i) = 1/n$ to each data index $i$. Generating one
draw from $\hat F_n$ is therefore exactly:

> pick an index $i$ uniformly at random from $\{1,\dots,n\}$ and report the value $X_i$.

That is the entire mechanism. And if we want a *whole new sample* of size $n$ from $\hat F_n$ —
$X_1^*,\dots,X_n^*$ — we repeat this independently $n$ times. Crucially, **the distribution
$\hat F_n$ does not change between draws** (it is a fixed object, computed once from the
original data), so each pick is from the same $1/n$-per-point distribution, independent of the
previous picks. Picking from the same fixed set, independently, again and again, is precisely
**sampling with replacement**: a value already chosen remains available and can be chosen
again.

::: {.callout-important title="The identification that makes the bootstrap legal"}
**Drawing a sample of size $n$ from the data with replacement is *identical to* drawing an
i.i.d. sample of size $n$ from the empirical distribution $\hat F_n$.** These are not two
analogous operations; they are the same operation described twice. This is why resampling is
mathematically legitimate rather than a heuristic: a resample is a real i.i.d. sample from a
real (estimated) distribution. Since $\hat F_n$ is close to $F$ (Glivenko–Cantelli,
@thm-gc), a resample from $\hat F_n$ behaves much like a fresh sample from $F$ — which is the
whole engine of the bootstrap.
:::

Sampling *with* replacement (not without) is forced by the requirement that the draws be
i.i.d. from a fixed distribution. If we sampled *without* replacement, removing each chosen
point, then the second draw would come from a different distribution than the first (one fewer
point, masses $1/(n-1)$), the draws would be dependent, and after $n$ draws we would simply
recover the original sample in a shuffled order — useless for assessing variability. With
replacement keeps the distribution fixed at $\hat F_n$ on every draw, which is exactly what
"i.i.d. from $\hat F_n$" demands.

## What a resample looks like {#sec-resample-picture}

A concrete consequence worth seeing: in a with-replacement resample of size $n$, some original
points appear multiple times and others not at all. How many are left out? The probability a
specific point is *missed* on one draw is $1-\tfrac1n$ (there are $n$ equally likely picks and
exactly one of them is this point, so the other $n-1$ miss it). Because the $n$ draws are
independent, the probability the point is missed on *all* $n$ draws is the product
$\bigl(1-\tfrac1n\bigr)^n$. As $n$ grows this tends to $e^{-1}\approx 0.368$. That limit is the
Calc-1 definition of $e$, but it is exactly the kind of identity worth re-deriving rather than
quoting. Take the log and read off a derivative:
$$
\ln\!\Bigl(1-\tfrac1n\Bigr)^{n}
= n\,\ln\!\Bigl(1-\tfrac1n\Bigr)
= \frac{\ln\!\bigl(1 - \tfrac1n\bigr) - \ln(1)}{1/n}.
$$
Set $h = 1/n$, so $h\to 0$ as $n\to\infty$. The right-hand side is
$\dfrac{\ln(1-h)-\ln(1)}{h}$, which (up to the sign of the step) is precisely the
difference-quotient defining the derivative of $g(u)=\ln u$ at $u=1$. Since $g'(u)=1/u$, that
derivative is $g'(1)=1$; the step here is $-h$, so the quotient tends to $-g'(1) = -1$. Hence
$\ln\bigl(1-\tfrac1n\bigr)^n \to -1$, and exponentiating (the exponential is continuous, so it
passes through the limit) gives $\bigl(1-\tfrac1n\bigr)^n \to e^{-1}$. So **about $37\%$ of the
original points are absent from any given resample**, and the rest appear with various
multiplicities. That
"$0.632$" (the complement, the fraction *included*) is a number that resurfaces all over
resampling-based model evaluation. @fig-resample shows the empirical CDF of the data alongside
the empirical CDFs of several resamples, all hugging it, plus the distribution of how many
times a point appears.

```{python}
#| label: fig-resample
#| fig-cap: "Sampling with replacement = sampling from $\\hat F_n$. **Left:** the original sample's empirical CDF (thick blue) with the empirical CDFs of 30 with-replacement resamples (thin grey) scattered around it — each resample is a genuine i.i.d. draw from $\\hat F_n$, so its staircase wiggles around the original. **Right:** across 5000 resamples of size $n$, how many times a fixed original point is included; the spike at 0 has height $\\approx (1-1/n)^n \\to e^{-1}\\approx 0.368$, the chance a point is left out entirely (red line)."
rng = np.random.default_rng(77)
n = 40
data = rng.gamma(2.0, 1.5, size=n)          # an honest skewed sample
xs0, Fs0 = ecdf(data)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.6))
grid = np.linspace(0, data.max() * 1.05, 300)
for _ in range(30):
    rs = rng.choice(data, size=n, replace=True)
    xr, Fr = ecdf(rs)
    axL.step(np.concatenate([[0], xr, [grid[-1]]]),
             np.concatenate([[0], Fr, [1]]), where="post",
             color=GREY, lw=0.7, alpha=0.5)
axL.step(np.concatenate([[0], xs0, [grid[-1]]]),
         np.concatenate([[0], Fs0, [1]]), where="post",
         color=POINT, lw=2.2, label=r"original $\hat F_n$")
axL.plot([], [], color=GREY, lw=0.9, label="30 resample ECDFs")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("probability $\\le x$")
axL.set_title("Resamples wiggle around $\\hat F_n$")
axL.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

# multiplicity of a fixed point (index 0) across many resamples
B = 5000
counts = np.empty(B, dtype=int)
for b in range(B):
    idx = rng.integers(0, n, size=n)
    counts[b] = np.sum(idx == 0)
vals, freqs = np.unique(counts, return_counts=True)
axR.bar(vals, freqs / B, color=POINT, alpha=0.85, width=0.8)
axR.axhline((1 - 1 / n) ** n, color=LINE, lw=1.8,
            label=fr"$(1-1/n)^n \approx {(1-1/n)**n:.3f}$")
axR.set_xlabel("times a fixed point appears in a resample")
axR.set_ylabel("relative frequency")
axR.set_title("About 37% of points are left out")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## A worked resampling estimate: the standard error of the median {#sec-se-median}

Here is the plug-in principle and resampling working together on a problem with no tidy
formula. We have a sample and we want the **sample median** as our estimate of the population
median — a plug-in estimate, $T(\hat F_n)$ with $T$ = "the median functional". Fine. But how
*uncertain* is that median? For the mean there is a clean formula for the standard error
($\sigma/\sqrt n$), but the median's sampling distribution has no such elementary closed form.

Resampling answers it directly, exactly because a resample is a real sample from $\hat F_n$.
The recipe — which is the bootstrap, owned and developed fully in the Inference track, shown
here only to demonstrate that $\hat F_n$ makes it *possible*:

1. Draw a resample of size $n$ from the data with replacement (= draw from $\hat F_n$).
2. Compute the median of that resample.
3. Repeat $B$ times, collecting $B$ resampled medians.
4. The spread (standard deviation) of those $B$ medians estimates the standard error of the
   sample median.

The logic is exactly Glivenko–Cantelli plus the identification box: variability *across
resamples from $\hat F_n$* mimics variability *across fresh samples from $F$*, because
$\hat F_n\approx F$. @fig-bootstrap-median carries it out on a real-looking skewed sample.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bootstrap-median
#| fig-cap: "Estimating the standard error of the sample median by resampling. From a single skewed sample of $n=60$ (light histogram, with its observed median marked), we draw $B=4000$ with-replacement resamples and record each one's median. The histogram of those resampled medians (blue) is the *bootstrap distribution* of the median; its standard deviation is the estimated standard error. There is no elementary formula for this number — resampling from $\\hat F_n$ supplies it directly."
rng = np.random.default_rng(404)
n = 60
data = rng.lognormal(mean=1.0, sigma=0.6, size=n)   # honest right-skew
obs_median = np.median(data)

B = 4000
boot_meds = np.array([np.median(rng.choice(data, size=n, replace=True))
                      for _ in range(B)])
se_med = boot_meds.std(ddof=1)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.5))
axL.hist(data, bins=18, color=FILL, alpha=0.7, edgecolor="white")
axL.axvline(obs_median, color=LINE, lw=2, label=f"sample median = {obs_median:.2f}")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("count")
axL.set_title(f"The original sample ($n={n}$)")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.hist(boot_meds, bins=35, color=POINT, alpha=0.85, edgecolor="white")
axR.axvline(obs_median, color=LINE, lw=2)
axR.set_xlabel("median of a resample")
axR.set_ylabel("count")
axR.set_title(f"Bootstrap dist. of the median\nest. SE $\\approx$ {se_med:.3f}")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"observed sample median      = {obs_median:.4f}")
print(f"bootstrap SE of the median  = {se_med:.4f}  (from B={B} resamples)")
```

The standard error printed under the figure was produced without a single distributional
assumption beyond "the data are i.i.d." — no normality, no formula for the median's variance.
That is the practical magic the empirical CDF unlocks, and the Inference track's bootstrap
article turns this demonstration into a full method (confidence intervals, bias correction,
when it fails).

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The empirical CDF is robust and assumption-light, but it is not magic, and an honest account
shows where it strains. This section is the line between "can compute a staircase" and "knows
when to trust it."

## It cannot see between, or beyond, the data {#sec-no-extrapolation}

$\hat F_n$ is flat everywhere except at the observed points and is *exactly* $0$ below the
minimum and $1$ above the maximum. So it asserts, flatly, that **no value smaller than your
smallest observation or larger than your largest is possible** — $P(X \le x)=1$ for all $x$
beyond the max. For a tail probability or an extreme quantile (a $99.9$th percentile from
$n=200$ points), this is not just imprecise, it is *wrong in kind*: the empirical CDF has
nothing to say about the tail it never sampled. It never extrapolates. If your question lives
in the tail — value-at-risk, flood levels, extreme losses — the empirical CDF is the wrong
tool and you reach for extreme-value theory or a parametric tail model. (Order statistics and
the limits of extremes are the subject of the companion article [PROB-23].)

## It is rough, and a smooth truth deserves a smooth estimate {#sec-roughness}

When $F$ is continuous, the truth is a smooth curve and $\hat F_n$ approximates it with a
jagged staircase. For the CDF itself the steps are usually harmless. But the *density* $f=F'$
cannot be estimated by differentiating $\hat F_n$ at all — the derivative of a step function
is zero almost everywhere and undefined at the jumps. If you want the density (the bell-shape
itself, not the cumulative climb), the empirical CDF gives you nothing usable, and you reach
for a **kernel density estimate** or a histogram, which deliberately smooth. @fig-edge shows
both failure modes: the empirical CDF flattening to wrong certainty in the tail of a
heavy-tailed sample, and the futility of reading a density off the steps.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability. **Left:** $n=150$ draws from a heavy-tailed (Pareto) distribution. The empirical CDF (blue) slams to 1 just past the largest observation, declaring larger values impossible, while the true CDF (red) still has real tail mass to the right — the empirical CDF is blind to the tail it never sampled. **Right:** the empirical CDF (blue steps) versus a kernel density estimate of the same data's *density*: you cannot recover the smooth density by differentiating the staircase, because its slope is zero between jumps. Different questions need different tools."
from scipy import stats

rng = np.random.default_rng(9)
n = 150
b = 2.5                                  # Pareto shape
data = stats.pareto.rvs(b, size=n, random_state=rng)
xs, Fs = ecdf(data)
grid = np.linspace(1, np.quantile(data, 0.995) * 1.4, 400)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.6))
axL.step(np.concatenate([[1], xs, [grid[-1]]]),
         np.concatenate([[0], Fs, [1]]), where="post", color=POINT, label=r"$\hat F_n$")
axL.plot(grid, stats.pareto.cdf(grid, b), color=LINE, label="true $F$ (Pareto)")
axL.axvline(xs.max(), color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.4, label="largest observation")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("probability $\\le x$")
axL.set_title("Blind beyond the largest point")
axL.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

# A second, lighter sample to compare ECDF vs KDE on the same axes is confusing;
# instead show ECDF (left axis) and KDE of the density (right axis) for clarity.
sample2 = rng.normal(0, 1, size=120)
xs2, Fs2 = ecdf(sample2)
g2 = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 400)
kde = stats.gaussian_kde(sample2)
axR.step(np.concatenate([[g2[0]], xs2, [g2[-1]]]),
         np.concatenate([[0], Fs2, [1]]), where="post",
         color=POINT, label=r"$\hat F_n$ (steps, left axis)")
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("CDF (steps)")
axR2 = axR.twinx()
axR2.plot(g2, kde(g2), color=ACCENT, lw=2, label="KDE of density (right axis)")
axR2.plot(g2, stats.norm.pdf(g2), color=LINE, ls="--", lw=1.4, label="true density")
axR2.set_ylabel("density"); axR2.grid(False)
axR.set_title("Steps give a CDF, not a density")
lines = axR.get_lines() + axR2.get_lines()
axR.legend(lines, [l.get_label() for l in lines], loc="upper left", fontsize=7.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## It needs the i.i.d. assumption it was built on {#sec-iid-needed}

Every result here — unbiasedness, the Binomial variance, Glivenko–Cantelli, and the legality
of resampling — rode on the sample being **i.i.d.** from a single $F$ ([PROB-08]). When that
breaks, so do the guarantees, quietly:

- **Dependence** (time series, spatial data, clustered observations). The indicators are no
  longer independent, so the variance formula @eq-variance is wrong (usually too optimistic),
  and a naive with-replacement resample destroys the dependence structure, badly
  underestimating uncertainty. The fix is a dependence-aware resample (block bootstrap) — a
  different object.
- **Non-stationarity** (the distribution drifts over the sample). There is no single $F$ for
  $\hat F_n$ to converge to; the staircase is an average of moving targets and estimates none
  of them.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The empirical CDF and the bootstrap are only as i.i.d. as your data.** The most common way
resampling lies is not a flaw in the math — it is feeding it dependent or drifting data and
trusting the resulting standard errors. With-replacement resampling *assumes* the data are an
i.i.d. sample from one distribution; if they are a time series, clustered, or non-stationary,
the resamples scramble the very structure that drives the real uncertainty, and you get
confidence intervals that are too narrow. Always ask "are these draws exchangeable?" *before*
you resample, never after.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the empirical CDF the right tool, and when is it the wrong one?"* The recitable
summary:

- **Use it** when you have an i.i.d. (or at least exchangeable) sample, you want a
  distribution estimate without committing to a parametric shape, your questions live *inside*
  the range of the data (central probabilities, the median, an interquartile range), and you
  want to resample to get standard errors for a statistic with no clean formula. In that
  regime it is unbeatable: assumption-light, consistent for any $F$, and the foundation of the
  bootstrap.
- **Do not use it** for tail/extreme quantiles beyond the data (use extreme-value theory),
  for the *density* rather than the CDF (use a KDE/histogram), or when the data are dependent
  or non-stationary (use dependence-aware methods). And remember it never extrapolates: it is
  silent about any region you did not sample.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived carrying four results and used each by name: the **CDF** as the universal
descriptor of a random variable ([PROB-02]); the **i.i.d. sample** ([PROB-08]); the
**Binomial as a sum of independent Bernoullis** ([PROB-11]); and the **convergence
vocabulary** ([PROB-21]) in which Glivenko–Cantelli is stated. This article turned the CDF
into something *estimable from data* and, more consequentially, into something you can *sample
from*. From here:

- **[PROB-23 *Order Statistics and the Uniform Distribution of Null p-Values*]** — the
  companion closing article. Sorting the sample turns $\hat F_n$ into the order statistics
  $X_{(1)}\le\dots\le X_{(n)}$; it derives the distribution of each, and the probability
  integral transform $F(X)\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)$ that makes a correct p-value uniform under
  the null. The two articles together are what the data tracks consume.
- **Across the track boundary — the bootstrap (Statistical Inference track).** This article
  proved the resampling is *legal*; the Inference track builds the *method*: bootstrap
  confidence intervals, bias correction, the percentile and BCa intervals, and the honest
  catalogue of when the bootstrap fails. The @sec-se-median demonstration here is a single
  frame of that full treatment.
- **Across the track boundary — bagging and resampling in learning (Classification,
  Unsupervised Learning).** Bootstrap aggregation ("bagging") and random forests draw their
  resamples from exactly the $\hat F_n$ defined here; the $\approx 0.632$ inclusion fraction
  from @sec-resample-picture reappears there as the "out-of-bag" sample used for free
  validation.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** V. Glivenko (1933) and F. P. Cantelli (1933) independently proved the
  uniform convergence theorem that bears their joint name. The quantitative companion is
  A. Dvoretzky, J. Kiefer, and J. Wolfowitz (1956), sharpened to its optimal constant by
  P. Massart (1990) — together the **DKW inequality** behind the uniform band in @fig-gc.
- **The resampling idea.** B. Efron, "Bootstrap Methods: Another Look at the Jackknife,"
  *Annals of Statistics* 7 (1979) — the paper that named the bootstrap and made the
  "sample from $\hat F_n$" identification of @sec-resampling the basis of a general method.
  Efron and Tibshirani's *An Introduction to the Bootstrap* (1993) is the standard book-length
  treatment.
- **The empirical-process view.** The proper home of Glivenko–Cantelli and DKW is
  *empirical process theory*, which studies the random function $\sqrt n\,(\hat F_n - F)$ as
  an object in its own right; its limit is the **Brownian bridge**, the gateway to the
  Kolmogorov–Smirnov goodness-of-fit test. A. W. van der Vaart's *Asymptotic Statistics*
  (1998), Ch. 19, is the graduate reference.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How do we turn the bootstrap SE of
  @sec-se-median into a confidence interval, and when is that interval trustworthy?* — the
  Inference track's bootstrap article. *What is the distribution of the sorted sample, and why
  are null p-values uniform?* — [PROB-23]. *Can we estimate the density, not just the CDF?* —
  kernel density estimation, in the nonparametric strand of the downstream tracks.
